threats.title: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal Missing isHooked Check Allows Credential Manipulation by Any Caller
threats.hypothesis_description: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal lacks the _hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked validation present in onDeposit and onTransfer. Any external address can call this function, triggering _tryValidateAccess which: (1) makes stateful CALL to role providers via _tryValidateCredential, consuming one-time-use credentials, (2) modifies _lenderStatus storage by unsetting expired credentials, and (3) can trigger arbitrary validateCredential calls on approved role providers. An attacker can grief lenders by burning their credentials or forcing credential state changes without any actual withdrawal occurring.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a missing access control check — a code defect where FixedTermLoanHooks correctly validates isHooked but AccessControlHooks does not. An attacker can call onQueueWithdrawal(victim, 0, 0, fakeState, maliciousHooksData) from any address to manipulate lender credential state, consuming one-time credentials or forcing credential expiration writes to storage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onQueueWithdrawal has NO _hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked check, unlike FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal (which checks at line 411-412) and AccessControlHooks.onDeposit (which checks at line 455). Any address can invoke credential validation and storage writes.

preconditions: AccessControlHooks instance deployed with at least one role provider
Target lender has an existing credential (expired or active)
If using validateCredential path: hooksData > 20 bytes pointing to an approved provider

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No msg.sender validation allows _tryValidateAccess → _tryValidateCredential → CALL to provider.validateCredential
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker crafts hooksData to call validateCredential on approved provider, consuming victim's one-time credential; or calls with empty hooksData when victim credential is expired to force unsetCredential write to storage
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can deny lender access to deposits/withdrawals by consuming their credentials, potentially locking funds in the market

economic_questions: Can credential consumption via validateCredential permanently deny a lender access?
Does forcing unsetCredential on expired credentials block subsequent refresh attempts?
Can this be chained with blocking to permanently lock lender funds?
validation_requirements: Confirm onQueueWithdrawal is callable by any address and proceeds past _tryValidateAccess
Test that _tryValidateCredential with crafted hooksData burns credentials on role providers
Verify _lenderStatus storage is modified when called from non-market address

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: lender fund lockup via credential denial
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Griefing attack - profit from market position manipulation

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When target lender has expired credential or one-time-use credential
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Can deny specific lenders access to their deposited funds

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any lender using AccessControlHooks markets with non-permanent credentials
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Temporary or permanent loss of market access, potentially trapping deposited funds
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All deposits in affected market for targeted lenders

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Role provider validateCredential side effects and credential consumption behavior
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify credential state changes persist in storage after attack

threats.title: Gradual APR Step-Down Bypasses Temporary Reserve Ratio Protection
threats.hypothesis_description: The temporary reserve ratio mechanism in MarketConstraintHooks allows a 25% relative APR reduction without penalty (relativeDiff <= 2500 returns originalReserveRatio). A borrower can exploit this by reducing APR by exactly 25% every 2 weeks, letting the temporary period expire each time. After each expiry, the new lower rate becomes the baseline for the next 25% reduction. Over 8 weeks (4 reductions), APR drops from 10000 → 7500 → 5625 → 4219 → 3164 bips (~68% total reduction) without ever triggering an elevated reserve ratio above the original.
threats.investigation_rationale: The 25% unpenalized threshold creates a loophole when applied repeatedly. Each reduction is measured relative to the CURRENT originalAnnualInterestBips (which resets after expiry), not the market creation rate. This allows systematic yield reduction while maintaining minimum reserves.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _calculateTemporaryReserveRatioBips returns originalReserveRatioBips when relativeDiff <= 2500 (25%). After 2-week expiry, delete temporaryExcessReserveRatio resets baseline, enabling repeated 25% reductions.

preconditions: Borrower controls market APR settings
Market has been active with initial APR
Borrower willing to wait 2 weeks between each step

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: relativeDiff = 10000 * (originalAPR - newAPR) / originalAPR; if <= 2500, no penalty
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Step 1: 10000→7500 (25% drop, no penalty, 2w wait). Step 2: 7500→5625 (25% drop, no penalty, 2w wait). Step 3: 5625→4219. Step 4: 4219→3164. Total: 68% reduction, zero penalty.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Borrower saves ~68% of interest payments over 8 weeks while maintaining minimum reserves, effectively extracting yield from lenders

economic_questions: What is the total economic impact on lenders from systematic APR reduction?
Can lenders withdraw quickly enough to avoid the reduced rate?
Does the fixed-term mechanism in FixedTermLoanHooks make this worse by trapping lender funds?
validation_requirements: Simulate 4-step APR reduction and verify no elevated reserve ratio at any point
Calculate total interest savings for borrower vs lender losses
Verify expiry correctly resets originalAnnualInterestBips baseline

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 68% of ongoing interest costs saved by borrower
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Proportional to market size and APR differential

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When lenders are locked in fixed-term positions
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Lenders receive significantly reduced yield without the reserve ratio protection intended to compensate

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders in the affected market
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 68% reduction in expected yield
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total accrued interest reduction across all lender deposits

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: 2500 bips threshold, 2-week expiry, baseline reset mechanics
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Full step-down simulation with actual _calculateTemporaryReserveRatioBips values

threats.title: Reentrancy via Stateful validateCredential CALL Enables State Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: _tryValidateCredential in both AccessControlHooks and FixedTermLoanHooks uses a stateful CALL (not staticcall) to the role provider's validateCredential function. LenderStatus modifications occur in memory but are not written to storage until after the external call returns (in _writeLenderStatus). A malicious role provider could reenter the market contract during this callback, triggering additional hook calls while the hooks contract's storage still reflects the pre-update state. This creates a window where the same credential could be used multiple times or state could become inconsistent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The external call pattern is: load status from storage → make CALL to provider → update status in memory → write to storage. A reentrant provider can exploit the gap between the call and the storage write.
deployment_assumptions: Borrower has added a malicious or compromised role provider that reenters during validateCredential
threats.config_evidence: Both AccessControlHooks.sol and FixedTermLoanHooks.sol use assembly CALL in _tryValidateCredential. The call passes gas() and invokes provider with full call capabilities.

proof_of_signal.pattern: assembly { if call(gas(), providerAddress, 0, ...) { credentialTimestamp := ... } } — stateful CALL to external provider before status is written to storage in _writeLenderStatus

preconditions: Malicious role provider registered by borrower
Provider implements validateCredential with reentrant callback to market
Market contract lacks reentrancy guard on the functions called by hooks
hooksData > 20 bytes to trigger validateCredential path

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: CALL(gas(), providerAddress, ...) with state update deferred to after return
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Provider reenters market.deposit → hooks.onDeposit → _tryValidateCredential reads stale _lenderStatus from storage
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Could set isKnownLenderOnMarket for unauthorized addresses or bypass deposit limits through recursive deposits

economic_questions: Does the market contract have reentrancy guards that prevent callback exploitation?
Can the reentrancy path create inconsistent isKnownLenderOnMarket state?
Can deposit limits or access controls be bypassed through recursive calls?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious provider contract that reenters market during validateCredential
Test whether market functions are reentrant during hook execution
Verify state consistency after reentrant calls

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Deployment cost of malicious provider contract
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.05 ETH in gas for complex reentrant transaction
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on market TVL — could bypass access controls for unauthorized deposits
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable — could enable unauthorized access to yield-bearing positions

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after provider is registered
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could compromise access control integrity for all markets using the hooks instance

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders in markets using the compromised hooks instance
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Unauthorized parties gaining yield access dilutes legitimate lender returns

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Market contract reentrancy guards, hook execution flow

threats.title: Cross-Market Credential Leakage Through Shared Hooks Instance
threats.hypothesis_description: When multiple markets share the same AccessControlHooks or FixedTermLoanHooks instance, the _lenderStatus mapping is global across all markets. A credential granted by a role provider for market A automatically validates for market B if both use the same hooks contract. While isKnownLenderOnMarket is correctly per-market, the credential validation in _tryValidateAccessInner checks the global _lenderStatus without any market-specific filtering. A lender approved for a low-value market automatically gains access to a high-value market using the same hooks.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code structure shows _lenderStatus is a single mapping(address => LenderStatus) without any market dimension. The credential check in _tryValidateAccessInner does not consider which market the credential was originally intended for.
deployment_assumptions: Borrower deploys multiple markets sharing the same hooks instance with different intended access policies
threats.config_evidence: AccessControlHooks.sol and FixedTermLoanHooks.sol: _lenderStatus is mapping(address => LenderStatus) without market dimension. _tryValidateAccessInner checks credential globally.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mapping(address => LenderStatus) internal _lenderStatus — single global mapping without market dimension. Credential from market A valid for market B.

preconditions: Multiple markets deployed using same hooks instance
Different intended access policies per market
Lender has credential from a role provider used by one market

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _tryValidateAccessInner reads _lenderStatus[account] without checking which market the credential was for
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Get approved for low-requirement market A → credential automatically valid for high-requirement market B
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Unauthorized access to premium/restricted lending markets with potentially higher yields

economic_questions: Do borrowers commonly deploy multiple markets with the same hooks instance?
Is there documentation recommending separate hooks instances for different access policies?
What is the yield differential between restricted and open markets?
validation_requirements: Confirm credential from market A grants access to market B when sharing hooks
Verify isKnownLenderOnMarket is correctly per-market
Test whether role providers are intended to be market-specific

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimum deposit amount for the less-restricted market
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Access to restricted markets with potentially higher yields

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient to meet minimum deposit on low-restriction market
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time multiple markets share hooks

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Borrowers expecting per-market access control; lenders in restricted markets expecting limited participation

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Deployment patterns — are shared hooks instances common?

threats.title: Gas Griefing via Unbounded Pull Provider Loop in Credential Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: _loopTryGetCredential iterates over all _pullProviders to find a valid credential. Each iteration makes a staticcall(gas(), providerAddress, ...) passing all remaining gas to the external provider. A malicious or buggy pull provider can consume excessive gas (without returning), causing the transaction to fail with Out-of-Gas. Even without malicious providers, a large number of pull providers increases gas costs for every credential validation, potentially making deposits and transfers prohibitively expensive.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop has no gas budget limit per provider call. staticcall(gas(), ...) forwards all remaining gas. A single problematic provider in the array can DoS all credential validation for all lenders across all markets using that hooks instance.
deployment_assumptions: Borrower has added a buggy or malicious pull provider
threats.config_evidence: AccessControlHooks.sol and FixedTermLoanHooks.sol: _tryGetCredential uses staticcall(gas(), providerAddress, ...) with no gas cap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: staticcall(gas(), providerAddress, 0x1c, 0x24, 0, 0x20) — passes all remaining gas to external provider with no cap. Inside loop over _pullProviders[].

preconditions: At least one malicious/buggy pull provider in _pullProviders array
Lender's credential validation falls through to _loopTryGetCredential
Provider consumes gas without returning a valid credential

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: staticcall(gas(), ...) with no gas limit per provider
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious provider enters infinite loop or expensive computation consuming all gas
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS all deposits, transfers, and withdrawal queueing for all lenders using the hooks instance

economic_questions: What is the gas overhead per pull provider call?
Can borrower remove the malicious provider quickly enough to prevent damage?
Is there a fallback for lenders if credential validation is DoSed?
validation_requirements: Deploy a gas-consuming pull provider and measure impact on credential validation
Test whether the 63/64 gas rule provides sufficient protection
Verify that removal of the provider is not also DoSed

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Deployment cost of malicious provider (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Zero per attack — provider passively DoSes by existing
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: DoS enables market manipulation while lenders can't act
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on market conditions during DoS
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persistent until borrower removes the provider

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During market stress when lenders need to withdraw
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete DoS of deposit and transfer operations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders using the affected hooks instance
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Inability to deposit, transfer, or queue withdrawals requiring credential validation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All deposits in markets using the hooks instance

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Gas consumption behavior of staticcall with gas() forwarding
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Measure remaining gas after provider DoS attempt

threats.title: HooksConfig.onBorrow Uses Incorrect Size Constant (RepayHook_Base_Size Instead of BorrowHook_Base_Size)
threats.hypothesis_description: In HooksConfig.sol, the onBorrow function's assembly block uses RepayHook_Base_Size (0x0224) instead of BorrowHook_Base_Size (0x0224) to calculate the calldata size for the hook call. Currently both constants equal 0x0224, so there is no functional impact. However, this is demonstrably wrong code — it references the wrong constant. If either constant is modified independently in a future update, the borrow hook calldata would be incorrectly sized, potentially causing the hook call to fail or read/write incorrect data.
threats.investigation_rationale: Code defect floor applies: using the wrong constant is demonstrably incorrect, regardless of current coincidental equality. The pattern 'let size := add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes)' in onBorrow should use BorrowHook_Base_Size.

proof_of_signal.pattern: let size := add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes) — should be BorrowHook_Base_Size. Constants BorrowHook_Base_Size (0x0224) and RepayHook_Base_Size (0x0224) are coincidentally equal.

preconditions: Currently no impact due to equal constant values
Would become exploitable if constants diverge in future code changes

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Uses RepayHook_Base_Size instead of BorrowHook_Base_Size in onBorrow calldata sizing
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If constants diverge: calldata truncation or buffer overflow in hook call
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Currently zero impact; future risk of incorrect hook execution affecting borrow operations

economic_questions: Are there plans to change either BorrowHook_Base_Size or RepayHook_Base_Size independently?
Could this affect hook execution if the calldata structures diverge?
validation_requirements: Confirm both constants are currently 0x0224
Verify the intended constant is BorrowHook_Base_Size based on the function context

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Zero current impact; maintenance hazard for future development

threats.title: MarketState.withdrawableProtocolFees Underflow Causes DoS on Fee Collection
threats.hypothesis_description: In MarketState.sol, withdrawableProtocolFees computes 'uint256 totalAvailableAssets = totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals'. If totalAssets falls below normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals (due to bad debt, token depreciation, or accounting edge cases), this arithmetic underflows in Solidity 0.8.x, causing a revert. This would DoS any function path that calls withdrawableProtocolFees, potentially preventing protocol fee collection and market state updates.
threats.investigation_rationale: The subtraction has no overflow protection (no satSub). In markets with bad debt or undercollateralization, totalAssets can legitimately drop below normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals, making this a realistic failure mode.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 totalAvailableAssets = totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals — unchecked subtraction that reverts on underflow in Solidity 0.8.x when totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals

preconditions: Market becomes undercollateralized (totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals)
Bad debt or borrower default scenario
Function path calls withdrawableProtocolFees

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Market undercollateralization through borrower default or bad debt
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS protocol fee collection; potentially block market state updates that depend on this calculation

economic_questions: Which market functions call withdrawableProtocolFees?
Can the underflow propagate to block critical market operations?
Is there a recovery path if this DoS occurs?
validation_requirements: Create scenario where totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals
Test which functions revert as a consequence
Verify whether satSub would be appropriate fix

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During market stress or borrower default
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could block protocol fee collection and potentially other market operations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol fee recipients, potentially all market participants if state updates are blocked
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Temporary inability to collect fees or interact with market
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Accrued protocol fees plus potential cascading DoS effects

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: totalAssets vs normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals in undercollateralized scenarios
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test underflow path and downstream effects

threats.title: Borrower Can Eliminate Fixed Term Protection by Setting endTime to Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: FixedTermLoanHooks.setFixedTermEndTime only checks that the new value is not greater than the current value (IncreaseFixedTerm check), but does not enforce a minimum remaining term. A borrower can set fixedTermEndTime to 0, immediately allowing all lenders to queue withdrawals since the check 'fixedTermEndTime > block.timestamp' in onQueueWithdrawal becomes 'false'. This eliminates the fixed-term guarantee that lenders relied on when depositing. Combined with APR reduction, a borrower could attract deposits at high rates, then immediately unlock withdrawals and slash rates.
threats.investigation_rationale: The monotonic decrease invariant is maintained (any value >= 0 satisfies it), but the economic intent of fixed-term protection is violated. Lenders deposit expecting locked terms, but the borrower can retroactively remove this protection at will.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (newFixedTermEndTime > hookedMarket.fixedTermEndTime) revert IncreaseFixedTerm(); — no minimum value check, allowing newFixedTermEndTime = 0

preconditions: Borrower controls the hooks contract (immutable borrower role)
Market has active lender deposits with expected fixed term
Borrower willing to act against lender interests (trust model violation)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set endTime to 0 → all withdrawals immediately unlocked → combine with APR reduction to extract maximum value
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Borrower attracts deposits at high APR with long term, then eliminates term and slashes rates

economic_questions: Can lenders detect and respond to fixedTermEndTime changes quickly enough?
Is there on-chain notification when term is reduced?
Does the temporary reserve ratio mechanism provide sufficient protection when combined with term elimination?
validation_requirements: Confirm setFixedTermEndTime(market, 0) succeeds
Verify all lenders can immediately queue withdrawals after
Test combined attack with APR reduction

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Proportional to loan size and rate differential

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After attracting maximum deposits at favorable rates
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete elimination of fixed-term guarantee for all lenders

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders who deposited expecting fixed-term protection
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of expected term-based yield protection
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Interest differential across all deposits for remaining expected term

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test setFixedTermEndTime(market, 0) and subsequent withdrawal queueing

threats.title: onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips Temporary Reserve Ratio Can Be Manipulated Through Repeated APR Changes Within Active Period
threats.hypothesis_description: When a temporary excess reserve ratio is active and not expired, a borrower can further reduce the APR below the original. If the new APR is below originalAnnualInterestBips but above or equal to intermediateState.annualInterestBips, the expiry timer is NOT reset (line: expiry = tmp.expiry). However, the reserve ratio IS recalculated based on the new reduction from the original. This creates a scenario where the borrower can reduce APR in small steps during the same temporary period: the reserve ratio recalculates (potentially decreasing compared to a single large drop), while the original expiry is preserved. After expiry, the original reserve ratio is restored, not the intermediate elevated one.
threats.investigation_rationale: The timer preservation logic (annualInterestBips >= intermediateState.annualInterestBips keeps old expiry) combined with the reserve ratio recalculation creates an asymmetry. The borrower can partially raise APR to preserve the timer, then the expiry arrives sooner, allowing faster baseline resets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (annualInterestBips >= intermediateState.annualInterestBips) { expiry = tmp.expiry; } — timer preserved on partial recovery, combined with reserve ratio recalculation from original

preconditions: Active temporary reserve ratio period
Borrower makes intermediate APR changes within the 2-week window

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Expiry preserved when APR partially raised, but reserve ratio recalculated — creates timing manipulation opportunity
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Step 1: Drop APR sharply (triggers 2-week timer). Step 2: Partially raise APR (preserves old timer, recalculates lower reserve ratio). Step 3: Timer expires sooner than expected for the actual APR reduction.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Borrower achieves lower effective reserve requirement for a significant APR reduction by gaming the timer preservation logic

economic_questions: What is the maximum reserve ratio reduction achievable through this timing manipulation?
How does this interact with the 25% unpenalized threshold?
Can this be combined with the gradual step-down for amplified effect?
validation_requirements: Simulate multi-step APR changes within a single temporary period
Calculate effective reserve ratio at each step
Compare to the reserve ratio from a single equivalent APR drop

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Reduced reserve requirement enables more borrowing from market
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Proportional to reserve ratio differential and market TVL

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During active temporary reserve ratio period
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Lower effective reserve protection for lenders

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders in the affected market
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced reserve protection below intended levels
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Borrowable assets freed by lower reserve ratio

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Timer preservation vs reserve ratio recalculation interaction
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model specific APR change sequences and resulting reserve ratios
